project_overview.project_name: Sudan SANAD - Emergency Crisis Response Safety Net Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To provide Emergency Safety Nets support to vulnerable and food insecure populations in selected areas of the Republic of the Sudan
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 602,000 beneficiaries (120,000 households) including vulnerable households in food insecure localities, with focus on female/child-headed households, households with children under 5, and households with disabled members

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on selected states and localities
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - extensive. Armed conflict between Sudanese Armed Forces and Rapid Support Forces since April 2023 has caused 11+ million internally displaced persons, 3 million cross-border refugees, severe economic contraction (GDP down 20% in 2023, 15% in 2024), and extreme food insecurity affecting 25+ million people. Project targets IPC Phase 3 and 4 areas with accessibility considerations

key_components.description: WFP-implemented unconditional cash transfers to vulnerable and food insecure households in IPC3 and IPC4 areas. Transfers of US$10 per person per month for 12 months (approximately US$50 per household per month), providing 50% of daily caloric needs. Delivery through multiple modalities: 65% cash-in-hand, 25% e-vouchers, 10% bank/mobile money. Targets 552,000 beneficiaries (110,000 households). Includes biometric registration, community-based targeting, and grievance redress mechanisms.
key_components.financing: US$66.3 million (cash transfers to beneficiaries: US$66.3M; delivery costs: US$11.9M; project management: US$6.8M)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Human Capital Sensitive Cash Transfers
key_components.description: UNICEF-implemented cash transfers targeting pregnant and lactating women (PLW) in relatively stable areas with functioning primary healthcare facilities. Transfers of US$30 per month for 12 months (US$45 for twins, capped per household). Targets 30,000 PLW beneficiaries. Linked to health services including antenatal visits, immunization, and nutrition support. Includes Mother Support Groups for social and behavior change communication. Covers behavioral change activities (US$0.8M) and delivery costs (US$1.5M).
key_components.financing: US$11.7 million (cash transfers: US$10.9M; behavioral change: US$0.8M; delivery: US$1.5M; project management: US$1.8M)

key_components.description: Financing for implementation costs including indirect support costs of WFP and UNICEF, operating costs of Sudan Country Offices, delivery mechanism setup, implementing partner and FSP contracting, beneficiary selection/targeting, biometric registration, stakeholder engagement, grievance redress mechanisms, environmental and social risk management, field office operations, and staffing salaries directly associated with project execution.
key_components.financing: US$22 million (WFP: US$6.8M; UNICEF: US$1.8M; plus delivery costs embedded in components)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: World Food Programme (WFP) and United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) as direct recipients and implementing agencies under third-party implementation arrangements. Each maintains a Project Implementation Unit (PIU) in Port Sudan with adequate staffing for day-to-day management, implementation, and M&E
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination Platform established within 30 days of signature with composition and terms of reference agreed by WFP, UNICEF, and World Bank. Core stakeholders meet frequently for overall guidance, coordination, and ensuring synergies. Coordination with other World Bank projects (SHARE, Somoud) to strengthen humanitarian-development nexus. WFP and UNICEF leverage existing partnerships with NGOs, CBOs, and implementing partners
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Alternative Procurement Arrangements (APA) - WFP and UNICEF follow their own respective policies and procurement procedures as assessed and approved by World Bank. Procurement includes consulting services, recruitment of consultants/firms/NGOs, goods, non-consulting services, cash transfers, and non-food items. Draft Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) and 12-month procurement plan required within 30 days of signature. Regular market assessments to manage FSP availability risks. Compliance with UN sanctions lists and World Bank debarment lists required
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: WFP and UNICEF responsible for M&E leveraging existing systems and processes. Quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) within 45 days of quarter-end. Quarterly project progress reports. Monitoring includes: beneficiary management systems, GRM, project monitoring dashboard, participatory process monitoring through partners/CBOs, financial reconciliation with audit trails, transparency of cash distribution, targeting accuracy monitoring, and grievance trends. World Bank contracts Bank Support and Monitoring Agent (BSMA) funded separately to independently monitor: (a) cash reaches intended beneficiaries on time; (b) community perceptions; (c) operational rollout. BSMA uses Geo-Enabling Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) where feasible. External audit through entity-level audit per Single Audit Principle

identified_risks.risk_description: Political and institutional fragmentation from ongoing war and historical instability severely affecting federal authorities and service delivery. Government apparatus relocated to Port Sudan. Significant civil servant displacement. Federal security apparatus functioning at state level but with severe limitations. Risk of political pressure on NGOs and implementing partners during community targeting
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular monitoring through BSMA for possible adjustments. WFP and UNICEF include screening elements in NGO partner selection ensuring accountability mechanisms for feedback and complaints. Coordination with UN agencies and international/local partners. Engagement with local administration to extent possible within OP/BP 7.30 framework

identified_risks.risk_description: High macroeconomic risk due to damage to banking sector and state treasury since conflict onset. Banking regulatory activity affected. Massive asset losses from looting and robbery. Low economic activity. Systemic risks magnified causing liquidity challenges in fragmented financial market. Hyperinflation (211% in October 2024), currency depreciation, disrupted supply chains. GDP contracted 20% (2023) and 15% (2024)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Proactive approaches by WFP and UNICEF: (a) distribution plans shared with FSPs ahead for planning; (b) payment/distribution instructions to FSPs prior to physical distribution; (c) engagement of non-traditional FSPs where necessary; (d) leveraging alternative transfer modalities (e-vouchers, mobile money) where feasible. Regular exchange rate monitoring and alerts to stakeholders. Contractual obligations in US Dollar to counteract exchange rate impacts. Strategic procurement reviews and long-term agreements for critical services

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Complex and dynamic operating environment may challenge implementing agencies' capacities despite WFP and UNICEF having adequate capacity and local presence. WFP drawing on SESNP experience. Sustainability challenged by ongoing armed conflict forestalling economic activities and causing infrastructure damage. Livelihoods opportunities expected to remain challenged. Worsening economic conditions risk real purchasing power of cash transfers
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WFP and UNICEF collaborate with capable implementing partners and contractors (NGOs, service providers, Community Committees) leveraging their experience and capacity. World Bank uses BSMA to reinforce M&E activities. Project invests in human capital and community/local institutional capacities to mitigate exclusion, address grievances, foster social cohesion, promote resilience, reinforce accountability. Enhanced safety net delivery tools serve as building blocks for national social protection system. Dependent on minimum security/stability levels

identified_risks.risk_description: High fiduciary risk from country environment impacting FM arrangements, potentially resulting in delayed reporting and inaccurate reconciliation. Unreconciled funds for long periods could result in misuse. Project supervision challenging in fluid context. Risks related to safeguarding funds and assets in insecure environment. Decentralized implementation in safer states with potential conflict escalation. Broader macroeconomic risks including hyperinflation and currency depreciation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close coordination between FM teams of WFP, UNICEF, and World Bank to ensure quality and punctuality of reporting and reconciliation. WFP applying corporate-led insurance policy throughout cash transfer distribution to safeguard funds and assets. Oversight mechanisms including monitoring, community feedback, gradual reintroduction of digital solutions. BSMA hired by World Bank to sample monitor and validate outputs/results. Quarterly IFRs within 45 days. Annual audited financial statements within 6 months. Internal audit reports sharing. Risk management process and condensed risk registers shared. Implementing partners audited per WFP/UNICEF policies

identified_risks.risk_description: High E&S risk from inherent project risks exacerbated by conflict context. Environmental risks include worker occupational health and safety (OHS) risks from cash transfer activities. Social risks include: (a) exclusion of vulnerable groups (ethnic minorities, IDPs, remote beneficiaries); (b) security concerns for staff, FSP workers, beneficiaries; (c) rejection of selection criteria; (d) weak community engagement risking elite capture; (e) conflict/tensions between host communities and IDPs; (f) illegal activities (violence, extortion, theft, armed assault, looting, vandalism); (g) unexploded ordnance risks; (h) GBV including SEA/SH; (i) intra-household tensions from cash transfers creating competition between spouses. High SEA/SH risk from conflict-affected areas with large IDP populations, weak GBV response mechanisms, power differentials from FSP/partner personnel, possible exclusion of women/girls
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WFP and UNICEF establish and maintain PIUs with qualified staff and resources for risk management. Contractors and implementing partners provide regular monitoring reports. Environmental risk management through OHS guidelines in Labor Management Procedures (LMP). Social risk mitigation through: (a) Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); (b) LMP; (c) Security Management Framework (SMF); (d) SEA/SH Action Plan; (e) Social Assessment (SA) with Social Risk Management Framework (SRMF). Gender-led communication using PHCs as platform. Training and deployment of 300 Mother Leaders. GBV risk-management plan development. Women and children as primary beneficiaries. Safe access mechanisms for women. Coordination with SHARE project on complementarities. Community committees inclusive of women, youth, minorities, displaced populations. Participatory targeting approaches. Functional GRM with free access

identified_risks.risk_description: High security risk from ongoing armed conflict with fighting rising in various areas. Evidence of grand robbery, thefts, and looting of life-saving aid by soldiers. Multiple and overlapping drivers on ground. Dynamic shifts within armed groups and militias causing intra-militia violence and fragmentation. Crossline and cross-border movement challenges. Bureaucratic impediments and politicization of aid. Military operations and violence toward civilians and aid workers. Widespread insecurity and lawlessness. Extortion and forced levies by armed groups. Commercial transporters fear going into contested areas
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design includes provisions to mitigate security risks to project-financed activities. Flexible geographic targeting allowing shifts based on security level changes. Autonomy given to frontline workers for adaptive management in dynamic conflict environment. Multiple alternative payment channels including new innovations. Coordination with telecoms for maintaining effective communications. Close working with SHARE project on data privacy at facilities experiencing looting/destruction. Regular security assessments informing implementation decisions

identified_risks.risk_category: Digital Network Coverage and Data Governance
identified_risks.risk_description: Digital and telecommunication networks severely impacted by conflict in different geographical areas, impeding communications and payment operations. Data governance weaknesses from facilities experiencing looting and destruction. Network limitations affecting digital payment modalities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WFP and UNICEF provide multiple alternative payment channels including innovations from other partners to ensure punctuality and fitness of transfers. Coordination with telecoms for maintaining effective communications. Gradual reintroduction of digital solutions. Close coordination with SHARE project on data privacy. Offline payment options and cash cards prioritized depending on network challenges. Flexibility in payment modality selection based on feasibility assessments

identified_risks.risk_description: Widespread displacement may lead to social tensions between IDPs and host communities. Risk of rejection of targeting criteria by beneficiaries. Weak community engagement could result in elite capture and exclusion
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WFP and UNICEF follow practical participatory approaches underpinned by community leadership from both host and IDP communities in targeting and monitoring. Community committees established/strengthened to be inclusive of women, youth, minority groups, displaced populations. Participatory verification approach engaging community committees in household-level targeting. Community leaders leveraged in designing communication messages and ensuring outreach. Women leaders mainstreamed across activities. GRM mechanisms consolidated through community member engagement. Feedback loops for adaptive management. Community sensitization meetings organized with implementing partners and social welfare/health/community structures

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk from sector policy implementation challenges in fragile context
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project builds on previous World Bank social protection initiatives (SFSP, SESNP) and lessons learned. Flexible and dynamic implementation modalities considering challenges across states. Adaptive management approach with programming flexibility. Coordination with other World Bank projects (SHARE, Somoud) for complementarity

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk from technical design challenges in complex FCV context including targeting accuracy, delivery mechanisms, and systems integration
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project builds on SESNP experience and regional/international evidence on cash transfer effectiveness. Dynamic design with flexible implementation modalities. Community-based targeting with clear eligibility criteria. Biometric registration systems. Multiple payment modalities. Regular market assessments. Participatory approaches. Detailed Project Implementation Manual (PIM) guiding implementation

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - comprehensive. Project-level GRM maintained by WFP and UNICEF allowing free access to alert on payment difficulties or malfeasance. WFP leverages existing GRM. UNICEF uses HOPE system for grievance handling. Regular reports on grievance/appeal receipt and resolution trends. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available at http://www.worldbank.org/GRS. World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (AM) including Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service available at https://accountability.worldbank.org. Community feedback mechanisms and social accountability tools. BSMA assesses beneficiary satisfaction with delivery
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: High gender focus. Women and children primary target beneficiaries. At least 50% of primary grant recipients expected to be women. Component 2 specifically targets 30,000 pregnant and lactating women. Gender-led communication using PHCs as platform. Training and deployment of 300 Mother Leaders as community change agents. Development of GBV risk-management plan as part of SEA/SH Action Plan. Mainstreaming gender across project spectrum. Mitigation of GBV/SEA/SH risks through early safeguard measures. Safe access mechanisms for women to mitigate GBV risks. Women leaders mainstreamed in community engagement. Addressing differential impact of conflict on gender. Recognition that women shoulder heavier burden of care and face higher abuse/exploitation risks. Targeting of female/child-headed households as priority beneficiaries
vulnerable_groups: Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - 11+ million in Sudan, 60% in urban areas, 50% in host communities, 34% in camps
Pregnant and Lactating Women (PLW) - 23% of population needing life-saving nutrition treatment
Children under 5 - 3 million acutely malnourished, 729,000 with severe acute malnutrition
Women and girls at risk of GBV, sexual violence, exploitation, abuse
Youth (15-24 years) - target of 300,000 youth beneficiaries
Refugees and cross-border displaced (3 million in Egypt, Chad, South Sudan)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed as FCV response. Armed conflict between Sudanese Armed Forces and Rapid Support Forces since April 2023 is central context. Project aims to: (a) protect human capital in short-term amid deep and persistent shocks; (b) smooth consumption for vulnerable populations including significant IDP share; (c) increase predictability of income/consumption; (d) maximize impact on resilience and behavior to avoid harmful coping mechanisms; (e) contribute to humanitarian-development nexus; (f) preserve hard-won development gains; (g) build resilience for future recovery. Targeting prioritizes areas exposed to both conflict and climate shocks. Flexible geographic targeting adapts to evolving conflict dynamics and accessibility. Community-based approaches foster social cohesion and reduce tensions between host and IDP communities. Participatory targeting and engagement build trust and acceptance. Project explicitly addresses conflict-related displacement, food insecurity, economic collapse, and service disruption. Coordination with humanitarian partners (WFP, UNICEF, UNHCR) ensures complementarity. Project operates under OP 7.30 (Dealings with De Facto Governments) given political instability
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sudan faces multiple, interconnected fragility drivers: (1) Armed conflict causing 11+ million IDPs, 3 million cross-border refugees, widespread loss of life and property, severe economic contraction (GDP -20% in 2023, -15% in 2024); (2) Extreme poverty rising from 15.2% (2014) to 56.7% (2024), with extreme poverty nearly quadrupling; (3) Severe food insecurity affecting 25+ million people (over half population), with IPC Phase 5 (Famine) conditions documented in some IDP camps; (4) Acute malnutrition affecting 3 million children, 729,000 with severe acute malnutrition; (5) Economic collapse with hyperinflation (211% October 2024), currency depreciation, disrupted supply chains, 46% decline in cereal production; (6) Service delivery collapse - over 50% households lack access to basic health services, healthcare facilities destroyed, disease outbreaks (dengue, malaria, measles); (7) Climate change as fundamental fragility driver - Sudan among most vulnerable countries, increased drought/flood frequency, 2024 floods displaced 117,835 people; (8) Institutional fragmentation - government apparatus relocated, civil servants displaced, weak state capacity; (9) Security challenges - widespread insecurity, lawlessness, extortion by armed groups, violence toward civilians and aid workers, access constraints; (10) Gender inequality exacerbated by conflict - women disproportionately affected, 75% of food insecure are children and women, increased GBV/sexual violence risks
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is central implementation challenge. Ongoing armed conflict with dynamic shifts in armed groups and militias. Evidence of grand robbery, thefts, looting of aid by soldiers. Crossline and cross-border movement deterred by violence. Bureaucratic impediments and politicization of aid. Military operations and violence toward civilians and aid workers. Widespread insecurity and lawlessness. Extortion and forced levies by armed groups. Commercial transporters fear contested areas. Project mitigation: (a) provisions to mitigate security risks to project-financed activities; (b) Security Management Framework (SMF) to be developed; (c) flexible geographic targeting allowing shifts based on security changes; (d) autonomy to frontline workers for adaptive management; (e) multiple payment modalities to reduce exposure; (f) coordination with security apparatus; (g) BSMA monitoring; (h) regular security assessments; (i) staff safety protocols; (j) unexploded ordnance risk awareness in some areas
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - extensive. As of October 2024: 11+ million internally displaced persons (IDPs) in Sudan; 3 million cross-border refugees (39% Egypt, 28% Chad, 27% South Sudan). IDP demographics: 54% women, 54% children under 18, 26% children under 5. Top IDP origin states: Khartoum (33%), South Darfur (19%), North Darfur (15%). Top displacement states: South Darfur (17%), North Darfur (14%), Gedaref (9%). 60% IDPs in urban areas, 50% in host communities (outside camps), 34% in camps/informal settlements. Displaced households report more severe/moderate malnutrition than non-displaced. Access to food is top humanitarian need among IDPs. Project specifically targets IDPs and host communities: (a) Component 1 targeting balances support between IDPs and host communities; (b) community-based targeting accounts for both populations; (c) targeting criteria include households hosting IDPs; (d) participatory approaches include IDP community leadership; (e) Component 2 prioritizes areas with high IDP concentration; (f) project aims to reduce tensions between host and IDP communities through inclusive engagement; (g) biometric registration captures displaced populations; (h) flexible targeting allows geographic shifts as displacement patterns evolve
